item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Second image has been removed due to being non-compliant with CC BY-SA 3.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That sound you just heard was my shudder. It's rare that an SCP gives me the proper creeps these days. I feel like I could almost understand if I thought about it hard enough… but I don't want to. Wonderfully creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know i am late but that won't stop me from reviewing: This SCP idea is amazing and fluid (especially interviews) but i have some ideas.
-What about adding this SCP to lore of 001 "When the day breaks" .
-Why they didn't send a class D with a modern diving suit and see what happens.
-Where was the remote control drone exploration (now a very commonly used procedure for SCP foundation )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely love it! I remember this one from time to time with admiration.
I think it might inspire something in a Call of Cthulhu game I'm planning on running in the future

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Imagine walking down the road on a foggy day only to see the top of a sub sticking out of a barely big enough puddle only 2 inches deep.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of my favorite SCPs. It's got everything- a fascinating introduction, mysterious details, disturbing imagery, and an interesting interview log.

item.title: Amazing
item.content:encoded:
This is definitely one of my favorites. The mystery build-up just keeps on going. Unlike many other SCPs, the victims actually seem semi-normal post-transformation and don't just clam up whenever they're asked anything, yet somehow you still managed to raise more questions than answers, culminating in that intensely eerie ending.

Solid +1.

item.title: Late Reply But...
item.content:encoded: You actually can via measuring DNA methylation levels, though it's approximate and correlates to biological rather than chronological age.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Woa O.o
Upvoted into oblivion

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved this skip. It is weird, mysterious, gets people acting a certain way simply because they have to work with as little info as they have… it is specially interesting how a great deal of what is going on is due to the victims that at first seem completely alien actually being completely mundane on how they think, and have to deal with their recent experience from there.

I would like to see some tales deriving from this, there is so many explanations and explorations to be done…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It took some digging, but I finally managed to dredge up the sources images.

The source photo for the B-Class boat was photographed by Herbert A. French and is in the Public Domain, so it remains unchanged.

The original background was a photo of Lakeside Funeral Home. The licensing on this image was, uh, ambiguous (at best), so I have replaced it with this photo by Hector Sanchez (CC BY 2.0).

The source for the diving suit photo could not be found (I had pulled it from the previous draft by MysteriousWanderer), but appears to be a recreation of a diving suit created by Pierre Rémy de Beauve. I've replaced it with this photo by girlinblack (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0) because it's the weirdest diving suit photo I found, and it has a similarly insectoid vibe as the previous image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Instant +1.

This article was well written, the details and the suspension was killing me through it. I really want more but eh, I guess I can't get everything I want! Heh.

item.title: U (Are DOOMED) Boat
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. These people think they are saving others from an anomalous phenomenon, but are really stuck in one themselves. And yet, who knows, perhaps in another dimension or timeline, they are saving people from that water world. The last interview was interesting, and the choice to have it be one D-class interviewing another who he was friends with really adds a lot of character.

I do sort of wish that the guy at the end lived when he took the suit off. What if they die if you forcefully take the suit off, but live if you can convince them to stay after the rain and take it off. That way, when dealing with this SCP, Foundation Agents would be in a similar boat (ha) with actual victims of the anomaly, each trying to convince the other "No, I'm trying to save you!" And each having their own experience telling them the other is wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hope this does. It's so well written but so unknown to people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. I've read this one a few times now. It's wonderfully terrifying, and it has an internally-consistent weirdness that manages to subvert ones expectations for maximum horror. It's very effective, and one of my all-time favorites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This should be brought back to the site's attention, story here is incredible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The most important question, then, is what happens to the discarded parts other than what the Foundation discovered inside the diving suit?

Also, were no studies performed on the recovered suit? Is it completely mundane?

item.title: Cerebrospinal fluid strikes again!
item.content:encoded:
Loving this in all its weirdness, also quite refreshing with how two-sided it is.

You get all this information but ultimately have learned nothing, and it keeps you wondering. +1 as always, Mister Ghost

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Found a fantastic pic of this on Deviantart.

item.title: Re: Anborough Approval Stamp of Keter
item.content:encoded: Bah dum, tss

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aaaaand there goes all the good feelings from SCP-666.5-j!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that bothered me too. What's with that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only problem I have (and I read the whole discussion to see if anyone else had noticed) is that, to the best of my knowledge, you can't tell age from DNA testing. So maybe mention that the DNA matched a victim of the skip known to be 8 years old?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is solidly in my top 10 favorites. Superb, PeppersGhost.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved it! These are the ones I enjoy reading the most.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This…this is…how do I even begin to show how great this is?

First of all, the effect itself happens at random, with no correlation, and is a weather phenomena, which may cause the actual danger, or may just attract the danger. Which is amazing.

Second, the most important part of an SCP which involves humans being being part of the anomaly is how they express whether or not they know about their effects. Usually, it's the generic, "I've had it since I can remember," "It happened suddenly one day," or, "I'm not gonna tell." But with this, the reason why they don't know how the anomaly works is because the new instances of the effect are the same as the old instances and know just the same amount of how the anomaly works. Except for the original, who is just plain insane. This is actually hilarious, because it means that the anomaly itself doesn't even understand what the hell it's doing.

Third of all, the way all the information comes together. Questions at the beginning were answered later on, which is kinda hard to do with something which isn't supposed to be explained. But at the end, reading the SCP actually gives more questions than answers, which is a pretty good thing to go for with an SCP. For instance, it is shown that in an alternate universe, everything is water, and the dead people have their eyes and teeth missing. The guy who took his suit off (who, by the way, was not trying to get other people to put on a suit, which brilliantly shows that it isn't some memetic effect) had his entire body made up of water, and a random pair of eyes and random teeth. It shows that the information is connected, but just tells it in a way which is, "what the hell, this information just confuses me more, whaaaat."

I give you my unofficial official unofficial GOLDEN UPVOTE OF GOLD*

  • contains trace amounts of pyrite (fool's gold), aluminum, and unicorn tears.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is chilling. Upvoted hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wonder if the whole thing about saving them from the blood rain is just a lie invented by the original captain to get more victims for his ship.

Either way great story-telling. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is bizarre, and the diving suits look like some kind of insect man.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was good enough for a plus one by the end of the first interview. By the end of the second, I could not upvote fast enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good writing, pretty creepy, and fairly original! I love it! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a strong and excellently told story. Upvoted, translated and upvoted again as a translation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice job man. Really nice. I like how you changed it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're pretty much my favorite, PG.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Spectacular rewrite. Has all the deeply-weird I love in an SCP with enough intimations of a logic and reason for it to keep me intently reading all the way to the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good concept, great first interview, fantastic second interview. I mean, I was thinking it was a little "here's what's exactly happening", but when the guy takes his suit off, it gave me chills.

Also, I like that the conning tower can appear out of large enough puddles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love you peppersghost. I really do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted.

An excellent rewrite. The interviews in particular were the highlight of the article, especially the second one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted.

Rest in peace, MysteriousWanderer, and know that your legacy will live on in this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creepy, but a little sad at the end. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved this one, also very creepy good job

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent one, especially the part about people putting their pets in the suits.

 "After a manifestation of SCP-1861 has ended, SCP-1861-A will disappear along with any remaining instances of SCP-1861."

I think it should say SCP-1861-B at the end here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wonderful absolutely wonderful. Damn magnificent job on the rewrite.
+1

item.title: Anborough Approval Stamp of Keter
item.content:encoded: This is just bloody great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. Yes. YES!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Christ almighty this is excellent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn creepy story, damn creepy photos. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me at "heavy precipitation and fog composed of saltwater, human blood, and human cerebrospinal fluid."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. Okay. That's… that's creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This just has that awesome feel to it that all the really creepy articles do.
Upvoted, happily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice SCP. Creepy, strange, and ambiguous.
+1

item.title: All ashore that's going ashore!
item.content:encoded:
OK, so this is a rewrite of The Crew of the SS Wintersheimer by MysteriousWanderer (account deleted).

It's very different from the original article, but since MW's version served as the inspiration for the current, I figure it's good to give credit where credit is due.

Discover more of my weird ramblings in
~PeppersGhost's Gallery of Illusion!~
